speaker: interviewer
text: [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: So after liberation, all three of you are together again. Continue on, if you will, please, about how you lived, where you lived, what conditions were. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, we took an apartment in, in the area. We didn't have any money. So at that time, at liberation, a lot of entertainment cafes opened up. And the Americans and the British soldiers had places to go and there was a lot of joy, and a lot of activity, and so on. And one of the things that a lot of cafes were doing, and I guess you call it today in the United States, karaoke type and contests. They had dancing and singing contests and things of that sort for money prizes. And I guess from my father being a cantor, I was blessed with a pretty good voice at that time, and I was a young kid. And my mother, since we had no clothes, we had absolutely nothing, had made clothing in the uniform style from blankets we found on the streets and blankets that the American GIs used to handout on the street. And so she made those little uniforms. And I looked, I guess, cute. And I had a pretty good voice, and I entered a lot of contests, singing contests. In these different cafes. And I suppose because of my age and because my voice wasn't too terrible I won a lot of those contests. So some of that money came in. I must also say that my sister and I, we didn't have a whole lot of pride at that point. We had to survive. And I guess today you'd call it begging, but on the street, we didn't mind asking for money from the GIs and-- for food, or chocolate, or anything of the sort.

speaker: interviewee
text: But still, we needed to pay the rent. For a while, we moved around a bit whenever the rent was due. Who cared? I mean, there were at that point, Belgians, Gentile Belgians, to me, were trash.

speaker: interviewee
text: What they had done, what they had permitted to do, what they had allowed the government to do, what these people were doing willingly. And many, many, many Jews, I had found out, were, were taken in by Belgians at that point [INAUDIBLE] that denounced them and so on. If I could take advantage of them and owe them money, that's exactly what we were going to do. And we did that for about four, or five, or six different places. We just ran from place, to place, to place until we decided, well, we have to eat and be a little more steady. And if our family is to find us again, we better be a little more permanent someplace. And, although I was quite young at the time, so was my sister, you were able to finally begin to get jobs. Not, not as we know it today, not applying for it, but in what we would call sweatshops today. And I did get work in-- I must have been about 11 years old-- in what they call a false jewelry sweatshop, where you worked by the piece. You only got paid on however many pieces you made. And I guess I developed a pretty good skill, and I was making a lot of pieces and I was making some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you continue at this false jewelry manufacturer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was my first of many jobs after that. I guess at the jewelery factory, maybe a year or so. Then just about anything that could come up. Odd jobs, odd work. Asking people if they wanted the sidewalk swept. Or, you know, things of that sort, menial jobs anywhere. So did my sister. Clean here, clean there, scrub the floors, anything that we could do just to make a little bit of money. Until one day I finally got a little bit lucky, finally. In-- I, I applied for a job in a textile factory. And for some reason, I was drawn to that and I seem to remember, you know, my father took me down to the textile factory, showed me many times how to make the-- how to work the machines, or at least show me not to work the machine, but how it was being done and so on. And it turned out that the owner of this textile factory that I applied to was once working for my father. And he was so elated in, in finding that somebody-- that I had survived, and that my mother had survived, my sister had survived, so on. He give me a job, took me under his wing, and groomed me, really, at the age of, I think I must have been 12 or 13 at that point, to work the factory. To-- I became a manager. I think I was about 13 years old. I went into sales, because he was also selling the, the items that he was doing. But to me, it was like deja vu. My, my father had shown me all of those things. And so I finally got myself a position.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this man had worked for your father earlier?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had worked for my father earlier and survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Isere Langman, who, by the way, I got to see about 10 years ago, I found him He didn't remember me, but that's another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked there for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- well, actually, until I left for the United States, which means probably four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: But previous to that, to go back some, the thing-- my, my mother, naturally, always insisted that I was a Jew and that I should believe again and so on. And, frankly, to this day, I, I will never know why this woman was still religious after losing what I've counted-- there were what I could count, 80 members of her family, to include five kids. It, it just was beyond me that she could believe in anything. And me, with having been torn from the little Jewishness that I had, into Catholicism and being a devout Catholic for really almost four years and going to mass daily and praying, I could never, never understand nor accept why either God, if there were two or one God, whether it was Catholic or Jewish or whatever, could let that have happened to my family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by that time I had also learned about the 6 million Jews that had perished, 1 and 1/2 million children that had perished. I said, how could any God-- one that I served, I thought, innocent children, innocent people-- how could it let that happen? So instead of either turning to-- continuing to be a Catholic, because I was right on through liberation, or turning back to a Jewish, I became a devout atheist. And I could never, never, never understand again about religion. I rejected it totally and completely.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, that hurt my mother a lot. And that continued right on through, probably today. Although today, maybe I won't call myself an atheist, maybe I'm a little agnostic right now. But I still question and I still am certainly not a Jew any longer. And that's the part-- that is the difficult part today. When they say that war is over, I don't think it will never be over for Holocaust survivors, and certainly not the hidden children of the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: You question daily, you wonder why, how could it have happened? People for 45 years now, before this first hidden children conference, have not let us complain.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we've learned not to complain, to suffer in silence, because we had to do that for three, four years anyway. We had to hold back our grief, and our, our weaknesses, and our crying, all of those things. It was very, almost easy, not to speak out. And for me, because of the fact that it, I guess it saved my life not to be Jewish, and not to sound Jewish, and not to look Jewish, it became very important to me after the war that I was never, never discovered as a Jew, to be pointed out as a Jew, or to be even different than anyone else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was very, very important to me, at first, in Belgium to speak French without being pointed out as a stranger, and later on when I came to the United States, to learn English in the best way I knew how not to be discovered.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to this date, I have a problem to, believe it or not, speak in public or being-- or, or have anyone listen in the hopes that they don't point out an accent, or they discover an accent. When I say I, I am not a Jew, I am. And if somebody discovers that I am a Jew or, or because of that ever made an anti-Semitic remark or call me today dirty Jew, certainly I am a Jew again. But I don't-- I don't feel Jewish anymore, nor do I want to. I wanted to assimilate. I wanted to be part of today's world without being pointed out. And I've, I've, I've questioned that so many times, but luckily with the Hidden Children Conference where other hidden children are now coming out and, and, and, and telling about their feelings and I hear that they feel the same way about it, whether it's out of fear of being discovered again or whether it's just a backlash from what happened, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I am certainly not religious any longer. Tell us more about when you came to America. And then we'll come back to the hidden children meetings that you have found.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, at first, I, I-- America was never something that I was aspiring to. In fact, I joined a Jewish organization, finally after the war called [NON-ENGLISH]. And they're, they're a Zionist organization. And at that age, I was still able to, to-- I mean, to-- for my character and my beliefs, to be formed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that group based?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in-- well, they-- they were quite big in Palestine at that time. It wasn't Israel. It was 1947. And in Belgium, in Brussels, they had a branch of [NON-ENGLISH] in Brussels. And in fact, many of the Jewish organization became quite known after the war, because they were also helping older Jews that they could find, all the survivors, putting it all together, giving them a sense of belonging. And and that time, they were in a fight for their-- the Zionists, at least, were in a fight for their homeland. And I was so taken by this, to finally be something and someone and belong to something, that I became quite the Zionist in the [NON-ENGLISH] and very, very active. And the whole purpose, at that time, was to get as many Jews from all over the world, survivors, to come to what was then going to be, hopefully, Israel. It was Palestine then, but we all knew that there was going to be a problem with that and with the Arab world. And that they'd have to fight for their statehood. And very frankly, at that time, fighting in wars was not exactly something that I wanted to go through again. But if that's all I had and I needed a sense of belonging, then that's what I would do. And the only surviving friend that I had before the war, who, who belonged to [NON-ENGLISH] with me, who did have parents that allowed him to go to Israel with his sister. And I wanted to go so badly, but my mother only had me and my sister. She wasn't about to lose another son too, to any wars or anywhere, and she wanted to stay in Belgium just in case my brothers and sisters would come back, would not allow me to do so.

speaker: interviewee
text: So to make a long story short, I forged her signature and I was pretty much on my way to Israel when she discovered that forgery. Went to the organization and told them about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was way underage. There was no way they were going to let me. And as fate would have it, my-- that friend of mine that I was going to go with and quite a few other people from [NON-ENGLISH] went from the [NON-ENGLISH] to the [NON-ENGLISH], and they were wiped out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So again, it seemed that I was saved. But I lost the only last friend I had from the war. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sammy. Sammy Felsenstein. So that was the story about Israel. Then my sister, who became a volunteer at the USO in Brussels, Belgium, met a-- an American soldier, an officer. That-- they fell in love. She was underage also. She was a little bit under 18. And he wanted to take her to the United States. And to her, that was salvation. That was the thing that she wanted to do badly. And the same situation with my mother was that she didn't want to lose a daughter. That's all she had. And here I was in then that was the three of us only. But she forged her papers. I guess we were quite good at forging papers. And she did marry him, and eventually came to the Unites States in 1948. We stayed in Belgium with the hopes that my sisters and brothers would come. And I went on to-- in that textile factory-- made a life for myself. And finally, my mother, in 1951, with the instance of my sister who had made-- well, actually, she had a misfortune too. The, the American soldier that she had married didn't turn out to be quite the person that she had met in Belgium. As it turned out, she had a baby with him. And he turned out to be quite the gambler, and an alcoholic, and so on. And so the marriage did not last very long. But she wanted my mother and to join her in the United States. I didn't want to go, I was making, finally, a life for myself. I loved what I was doing. I had, finally, almost a career, I was making good money. I had a life. And I told my mother, why doesn't she go, see what it's like, with the hope that she wouldn't like it and come back. And then I'll join the later.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in 1951, my mother joined my sister. And I went into a-- what is best described as an orphanage of boys and girls that were waiting. They were all orphans. I mean, they had lost their fathers and mothers in, in the war, in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were waiting to-- for their quota to immigrate either to Israel, or to Canada, or to the United States. And there were about 50, 50 of us in that orphanage. And that's when-- being underage and I was a little bit under 16, my mother made the papers for me to join her in the United States, and I had to come. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So while you're in this orphanage, you continued to work in your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I, I did. In fact, I was one of the sole support of that orphanage because I was doing quite well and all the money that I was making was going into the orphanage and-- for use for the other unfortunate other children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year did you join your mother and sister in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, January 5th, 1952 was the day that they dragged me-- [LAUGHS] --to the United States. And I probably had the worst six months of my life over here because it was like a new beginning again. I didn't speak English, and that was very, very dramatic for me. I-- as I told you, it was very important to assimilate. I didn't want to be pointed out as a stranger or an immigrant. I did not have a real-- I had a trade, but in the Unites States I found out that the unions were very strong, especially in the textile factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to apply in the textile factory, you had to be part of a union. To be part of a union, you had to have a job in a textile factory. So that didn't work out. And not knowing the language, not having a trade I could get into, I had to start my life all over again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not being able to speak English, I had to find something that didn't require the language. So to make a long story short, I started my life over as a delivery boy for a grocery store chain.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city was this in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York. In fact, my sister, when she first came to the United States, lived in Philadelphia and then went on to New York. And that's what my, my first view of the United States was, New York City, in Manhattan. And I became, as I said, a delivery boy. And from that, I mean, it was such a drastic change to what I had built for myself again, that I decided that I, I just wasn't going to stay here. And six months later I had a ticket to go back to Belgium and I was going to go back to my job, and what I knew best, and so on. And that's when I-- the last thing that I did, somebody had told me about a, a French ball, a dance that was being held at the Statler Hotel in New York. And how wonderful it would be to just go there and see if by any chance anyone that I knew before the war would be there, because a, a French ball, which was also attended by some Belgians, that would be a good place to, to see if any, any anybody would have survived this. Because the one thing that I did not have, to go back some, is that anyone that was hidden in orphanages and convents and in group situation, at least after the war, they had somebody that they knew during the war that they reunited with and they had their own support group that way. Having been hidden in a single family and alone, I had no one. So I, I, I had nothing and nobody, nobody that I knew, nobody that I could've made friends with or survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to that French ball and it turned out that I did meet somebody that knew a mutual friend I knew-- that I had met right after the war liberation time, who is still alive today. It's my only friend from Belgium. He was my age and he went through pretty much the same thing I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Although we, having worked together-- hidden children, the unusual thing is that we never spoke about, our hiding experience. That was the most unusual thing, and I only discovered that later, that how could we have worked and known each other now for 45 years and having worked together for about nine years, and we never spoke about our hidden experience. But neither did I do it with my sister, so-- but that's another story. Anyway, I, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Oh, yes. After--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, with this ticket you had six months after arriving in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. No, because I met that person who told me about a-- my-- that friend of mine. And I called him, we got together. And he says, why don't you give yourself a chance? Now you have a friend here. There's also a Jewish organization that he belonged to called [NON-ENGLISH] in New York, that you'll have a sense of belonging. And he was working then for his uncles, who owned a machine shop supply distributorship.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, who knows? Maybe I'll talk to them and you could work with me and so on. Why don't you give yourself a chance? To make a long story short, I, I did join [NON-ENGLISH]. He introduced me then to my first wife, who became my first wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had someone again, and I had a group of some Jewish children that had lost their parents in the Holocaust. And so I had a sense of belonging. I had people that I could commiserate with that would understand the situation. He did get me an interview and a job with his uncles. So I became a shipping delivery boy again. And then I joined the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married, at this point, to your first wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- we had met. We had--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if I had came in-- if I had come in 1952, I assume I met her right that year. 1952, 1953. And we started going out, as they called it then, steady. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, I then wanted to do something with my life. And in those days, when they had the draft and selective service, very few careers could begin because they would also ask you-- they would always ask you, how do you stand with the draft?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so if you had to be called at any time, and in that-- and that was just about the Korean War, the Korean conflict was still going on. And certain things in Israel were happening in Lebanon and so on we're going on. So they were very much afraid to start you off on a career and lose you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, well, if I can't start-- I, I just can't go on being a shipping boy in a, in a grocery store. Why don't I then join the American army and go into-- take on this much schooling as I can and get rid of it, and then be able to apply for at least a career?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which I did, but in those days, you had to join for three years if you enlisted. Then came a couple of other things that, that tore my life apart. It was the end of the Korean conflict, as I said. It was just beginning to end. And at that time, there was a good chance of my winding up in Korea right after the basic training, which, because of some background that they understood, they decided, with the story that I was giving them, that maybe this would not be a good idea. And it's unusual to have the American Army understanding that. But then came another, another thing that, that happened, that was the most amazing thing. I went to a school that I had a chance to go to, field radio repair. And upon graduation, you were always sent someplace else to put your skills at use. And I got my orders to go to Germany. And I must tell you, in 1956, the wounds were still very much open. And my hatred for Germans was very much there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had very mixed feelings about that. And I, I said, Germany? The place that I promised myself I would never see or never visit. Not in my lifetime. I said, well, why not? Maybe, being in the army, I can extract some revenge. And I was very bitter, I still was very bitter in those days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to speak about it to then-- I did-- I had married in 1957. And it happened right after marriage. And I spoke to my wife, who, by the way, had not found out yet that she also had been a hidden child. It's another story. She thought that she belonged to the family that was her mother and father, they-- she thought biological, but she found out that her mother and father had been shot in Poland, and that she had been adopted by the family, but too young to know and found out later. That-- well, the trauma of finding out at 18 or 19, I think she was, is what kind of broke up our marriage. But that's-- I'm digressing now. Anyway, my-- I spoke about it with my family, about going to Germany. And I-- they, they dissuaded me to do that they said, because the things that you might feel, the things that you might do, although you could get away with things like that during the war, is not something that you can do or get away with in peacetime. And those things wouldn't be accepted. And to make a long story short, I went to my company commander and told them that, I'll go. I'm under orders to go, but the consequences, they'll have to understand. I don't know what my feelings would be, I don't know what my emotions will make me do, but I don't think it was a good idea. And they felt the same way. So I did not go to Germany. But then came another, another situation, which is like the world goes around. During a, a bombing in Belgium by the allied forces, it was actually the American bombing, when I ran for shelter under a doorway I fell and I had broken my front tooth. And that stayed with me, of course, through the war and after the war. And while I was in the army the front tooth started to react, the nerves started to react, and it started to darken and become black. And just as a-- as a funny little story, is that the, the thing that the American soldiers, or I should say the American Air Force, had done and broken, the American army fixed for me. And I'm only saying that because while I was sitting there explaining it to the dentist, who was an officer, I told them, well, it took you about 20 years, but you're fixing something that you people did to me. And then, of course, he was interested in the story and I went on from there. But what happened just when I was about to be discharged in the army, it was 1958, and the Lebanese conflict happened with Israel and so on. And I was-- I had an [? MOS, ?] a job in the army that was very, very tough to do and tough to get. And about a week before I was to be discharged, my company and what I was doing was necessary in Lebanon, in case the conflict expanded. The Security Council was meeting at the time about the situation there, and it was Lebanon against Israel. And I, as I said, had become, finally, maybe a Jew again and a Zionist. And my choice was not necessarily the United States anyway. The American Army was-- became-- was something I needed to do, not that I wanted to do. Israel was still very much on my mind, and something I really believed in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went. And it was, as we were told, which way the guns were to face. And it was against the Israeli Army at the time. It was a choice to be made again as to what would I do faced with a situation where you have to actually defend the American Army against an Israeli Army, and defend Arabs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at the time, of course, being a Zionist, I know the situation what had happened in 1948 in Israel. So again, a decision to be made. And again, the possibility of who knows? So I was spared because Security Council voted for a ceasefire, and we came home and I was discharged.
